column17: 1. **Verify EDR Deployment on Endpoints:** - Access each relevant endpoint (e.g., EC2 instances, on-premises servers, workstations, mobile devices) via SSH or RDP. - Confirm that approved EDR software is installed and actively running on each endpoint by checking the system tray, services, or using specific commands related to the EDR solution. 2. **Check EDR Configuration:** - Review the configuration of the EDR agent on each endpoint or access the centralized EDR management console. - Ensure the EDR is set to monitor, detect, and alert on security threats according to your organization's policy guidelines. 3. **Validate EDR Functionality:** - Simulate a security incident (e.g., introduce a benign test virus) to check if the EDR system generates real-time alerts and incident reports as expected. - Review logs from the EDR solution to confirm it is actively detecting and responding to threats. 4. **Document Findings:** - Record the status of EDR deployment, configuration, and functionality for each endpoint. - Note any discrepancies or areas for improvement in the EDR system's performance.

column4: Ensure installation of the AWS Replication Agent
column5: Set up and verify the installation of the AWS Replication Agent on all relevant systems to facilitate efficient and reliable data replication. This process includes downloading the agent, configuring it according to best practices, and ensuring it is correctly integrated with your AWS environment. Regularly check the agent’s performance and update it as needed to maintain optimal functionality and data integrity during replication processes.
column6: Installing the AWS Replication Agent is crucial for enabling efficient and reliable data replication, ensuring that critical data is accurately duplicated across systems. Proper configuration and integration with your AWS environment optimize the agent’s performance, enhancing data availability and disaster recovery capabilities. Regular checks and updates of the replication agent help maintain its effectiveness, ensuring data integrity and minimizing the risk of replication failures.
column7: 1. On the source servers page, from Actions, choose add servers to obtain the agent installer link. 2. On your source server (in our case, the EC2 instance that was already created) download the appropriate agent installer for your operating system. o For Linux instance on US-East-1. Substitute your region in the {Region} brackets of this command: wget -O ./aws-replication-installer-init https://aws-elastic-disaster-recovery-us-east-1.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/latest/linux/aws-replication-installer-init 3. Run following command: chmod +x aws-replication-installer-init; sudo ./aws-replication-installer-init 4. Type in your region. Region is case sensitive: if you’re in us-east-1, make sure you type “us-east-1”. 5. If you’re using SSH, you will be prompted with your activation ID and secret activation key. Make sure you have those accessible for the IAM user you’re using. You can generate a new key from the IAM dashboard if you forgot to save your key. 6. Select “Enter” to replicate all servers. 7. All servers should replicate. 8. Make sure your OS is up to date. If you run into an error replicating your devices, view the documentation on troubleshooting the AWS replication installation here: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/mgn/latest/ug/installation-requirements.html. 9. If install runs successfully, the source server will appear in your Elastic Disaster Recovery Console dashboard on the “source servers” page. This will signify the beginning of the replication process.

column4: Ensure execution of a failback
column5: This method involves transitioning operations back from the backup or recovery system to the primary system after the resolution of a disruption or disaster. You can execute a failback either to the original server, ensuring continuity and restoring the previous state, or to a new server, which might be necessary if the original server is compromised or no longer functional. The failback process ensures that all updated data and configurations are transferred back, maintaining the integrity and functionality of the primary system.
column6: A failback is crucial for restoring normal operations after a disaster recovery scenario. Transitioning operations back to the primary system ensures continuity and leverages the original environment's configurations and settings. This process can be directed either to the original server, maintaining the existing infrastructure, or to a new server if the original is compromised. Ensuring all data and configurations are accurately transferred back preserves system integrity and functionality, reducing downtime and allowing the organization to resume normal operations efficiently. Impact: Failback Prerequisites: • The volumes on the server you are failing back to are the same size or larger than the recovery instance if failing back to a new server. • The failback client has the proper permissions to access both Elastic Disaster Recovery and S3 services on TCP port 1500 inbound and TCP port 443 outbound to communicate with the failback client. • A public IP is added to the recovery instance.
column7: Performing the failback: 1. Download the failback client ISO 2. Attach the ISO to your original server and boot up the server. o The failback client will prompt for the IAM access key and secret key generated when making the user with the permission to access the failback. It will also ask for the region of the recovery instance. Remember: regions are case sensitive. If you’re in US east 1, type “us-east-1.” o If you are failing back to the original server, the failback client will automatically detect the recovery instance and map the data volumes for replication. o If you are failing back to a new server, you may need to manually specify from a list of available recovery instances and map the data volumes. o The failback client will verify that the chosen recovery instance has connectivity to the Elastic Disaster Recovery service. o The replication software will be downloaded to the failback client and then configured. Connectivity will be made between the failback client and the replication agent on the recovery instance to begin data replication. 3. Return to the elastic disaster recovery console and recovery instances to see the current state of replication. Failing back to the original server will show “rescan” in the console, while failing back o a new instance will perform an “initial sync.” 4. After the data replication is completed, you will be able to perform the failback. o Check the state of the recovery instance to ensure that it’s ready to complete a failback. o Select your recovery instance, then choose failback for the chosen recovery instance(s). o Choose failback again the complete a failback for the chosen recovery instance(s). During the failback process, the failback client will prepare your source server for normal operation. After it has completed successfully, the failback client will return “failback completed successfully” in the console. 5. Reboot the server and return to normal operations. 6. Clean up failback job; terminate recovery job by following the steps outlined above when we ran a drill.

column4: Maintain current contact details
column5: Ensure contact email and telephone details for AWS accounts are current and map to more than one individual in your organization. An AWS account supports a number of contact details, and AWS will use these to contact the account owner if activity judged to be in breach of the Acceptable Use Policy or indicative of a likely security compromise is observed by the AWS Abuse team. Contact details should not be for a single individual, as circumstances may arise where that individual is unavailable. Email contact details should point to a mail alias which forwards email to multiple individuals within the organization; where feasible, phone contact details should point to a PABX hunt group or other call-forwarding system.
column6: If an AWS account is observed to be behaving in a prohibited or suspicious manner, AWS will attempt to contact the account owner by email and phone using the contact details listed. If this is unsuccessful and the account behavior needs urgent mitigation, proactive measures may be taken, including throttling of traffic between the account exhibiting suspicious behavior and the AWS API endpoints and the Internet. This will result in impaired service to and from the account in question, so it is in both the customers' and AWS's best interests that prompt contact can be established. This is best achieved by setting AWS account contact details to point to resources which have multiple individuals as recipients, such as email aliases and PABX hunt groups.

column4: Ensure your WorkSpaces image has the appropriate CIS Operating System Benchmark applied
column5: Utilize the CIS Benchmark to secure the Operating system image that you are utilizing for your WorkSpaces.
column6: Securing the Operating system with a CIS Benchmark ensures all systems remain in a secure, compliant and hardened state.
column7: Spin up a WorkSpaces instance and run a manual assessment by confirming the CIS Operating System Benchmark recommendations for the applicable operating system are applied. column8: Perform the steps below using the downloaded free version of the applicable CIS Operating System Benchmark and manually apply the recommendations for the WorkSpaces instance. column29: 1. **Launch a WorkSpaces Bundle:** - Navigate to the AWS Management Console and access the WorkSpaces service. - Create a new WorkSpaces bundle using the desired operating system. 2. **Access the WorkSpace:** - Once the WorkSpace is provisioned, connect as an Administrator using SSH for Linux or RDP for Windows. 3. **Apply the CIS Benchmark:** - Download the applicable CIS Operating System Benchmark from the CIS website. - Manually apply the benchmark recommendations, which may include adjusting security settings, user permissions, and other configurations. 4. **Verify Compliance:** - Conduct a manual assessment of the WorkSpaces instance against the CIS Benchmark to ensure all recommendations are applied. - Optionally, use a certified third-party tool to assist in the assessment process. 5. **Create a Secure Workspace Bundle:** - After applying the benchmark settings, create a new WorkSpaces bundle that includes the secure configurations for future use.

column8: Perform the steps below to create or edit the IP Allow list for WorkDocs From the Console: 1. Log into the AWS console. 2. column10: IP Lists currently only work for IPv4 addresses and denying access through an IP list is not supported.
column15: The task of reviewing and modifying the IP Allow List for AWS WorkDocs is currently a manual process because AWS WorkDocs does not support direct management of IP Allow Lists through boto3 or AWS CLI. column17: 1. **Log into the AWS Console:** Navigate to the AWS Management Console and log in with appropriate credentials. 2. **Go to WorkDocs:** Access the WorkDocs Console by navigating to https://console.aws.amazon.com/zocalo/. 3. **Open Admin Control Panel:** Under the "My Account" section, select "Open admin control panel". 4. **Access IP Allow List Settings:** Look for the section labeled "IP Allow List" and select "Change" to view existing configurations. 5. **Review IP Address Ranges:** Check the list of allowed IP address ranges and any individual IP addresses listed. Ensure that they align with trusted networks as per your organizational policy and security posture. 6. **Confirm Compliance:** If the displayed IP address ranges do not match your organization's trusted networks, refer to the remediation steps provided to create or amend the IP Allow list as necessary. 7. **Remediation (if needed):** To allowlist, enter the appropriate CIDR values for acceptable IP addresses in the "Enter CIDR value" input field. For individual IPs, use /32 as the CIDR prefix, then click "Add" followed by "Save Changes" to apply the configuration.

column4: Ensure security questions are registered in the AWS account
column5: The AWS support portal allows account owners to establish security questions that can be used to authenticate individuals calling AWS customer service for support. It is recommended that security questions be established.
column6: When creating a new AWS account, a default super user is automatically created. This account is referred to as the 'root user' or 'root' account. It is recommended that the use of this account be limited and highly controlled. During events in which the 'root' password is no longer accessible or the MFA token associated with 'root' is lost/destroyed it is possible, through authentication using secret questions and associated answers, to recover 'root' user login access.

From the drop-down menu Click My Account 5. In the Configure Security Challenge Questions section on the Personal Information page, configure three security challenge questions. 6. Click Save questions .

Scroll down to the Configure Security Questions section 5. Click on Edit 6. Click on each Question • From the drop-down select an appropriate question • Click on the Answer section Internal Only - General • Enter an appropriate answer o Follow process for all 3 questions 7. Click Update when complete 8. Save Questions and Answers and place in a secure physical location

column3: CIS_Amazon_Elastic_Kubernetes_Service__EKS__Benchmark_v1_5_0_PDF
column4: Ensure that the kubelet kubeconfig file ownership is set to  root:root
column5: If kubelet is running, ensure that the file ownership of its kubeconfig file is set to root:root.
column6: The kubeconfig file for kubelet controls various parameters for the kubelet service in the worker node. You should set its file ownership to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be owned by root:root.
column7: Method 1 SSH to the worker nodes To check to see if the Kubelet Service is running: sudo systemctl status kubelet The output should return Active: active (running) since.. Run the following command on each node to find the appropriate kubeconfig file: ps -ef | grep kubelet The output of the above command should return something similar to --kubeconfig /var/lib/kubelet/kubeconfig which is the location of the kubeconfig file. Run this command to obtain the kubeconfig file ownership: stat -c %U:%G /var/lib/kubelet/kubeconfig The output of the above command gives you the kubeconfig file's ownership. Verify that the ownership is set to root:root. Method 2 Create and Run a Privileged Pod. You will need to run a pod that is privileged enough to access the host's file system. This can be achieved by deploying a pod that uses the hostPath volume to mount the node's file system into the pod. Here's an example of a simple pod definition that mounts the root of the host to /host within the pod: Internal Only - General apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: file-check spec: volumes: - name: host-root hostPath: path: / type: Directory containers: - name: nsenter image: busybox command: ["sleep", "3600"] volumeMounts: - name: host-root mountPath: /host securityContext: privileged: true Save this to a file (e.g., file-check-pod.yaml) and create the pod: kubectl apply -f file-check-pod.yaml Once the pod is running, you can exec into it to check file ownership on the node: kubectl exec -it file-check -- sh Now you are in a shell inside the pod, but you can access the node's file system through the /host directory and check the ownership of the file: ls -l /host/var/lib/kubelet/kubeconfig The output of the above command gives you the kubeconfig file's ownership. Verify that the ownership is set to root:root.
column8: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on each worker node. For example, chown root:root
column15: The task requires checking the file ownership of the kubeconfig file on EKS worker nodes, which involves accessing the file system of EC2 instances. This cannot be directly automated using AWS APIs, boto3, or AWS CLI as they do not provide file system access. While AWS Systems Manager could potentially automate command execution, it still requires manual setup and configuration, making the process inherently manual for compliance verification.
column17: 1. **SSH Access to Worker Nodes:** - Connect to each worker node in your EKS cluster using SSH with the appropriate permissions and SSH key. - Command Example: `ssh -i /path/to/your/key.pem ec2-user@<worker-node-ip>` 2. **Check if Kubelet Service is Running:** - Once connected, verify the kubelet service status. - Command: `sudo systemctl status kubelet` - **What to verify:** Ensure the output indicates "Active: active (running)". 3. **Find the Kubelet Kubeconfig File:** - Identify the kubeconfig file path used by kubelet. - Command: `ps -ef | grep kubelet` - **Expected output:** Look for the `--kubeconfig` flag, typically pointing to `/var/lib/kubelet/kubeconfig`. 4. **Check the File Ownership of the Kubeconfig File:** - Verify the ownership of the kubeconfig file. - Command: `stat -c %U:%G /var/lib/kubelet/kubeconfig` - **Expected secure configuration:** Ownership should be `root:root`. 5. **Document the Findings:** - Record the outputs from your commands for audit purposes, including kubelet status, kubeconfig file path, and its owner.

column3: CIS_Amazon_Elastic_Kubernetes_Service__EKS__Benchmark_v1_5_0_PDF
column4: Ensure that the --authorization-mode argument is not set to  AlwaysAllow
column5: Do not allow all requests. Enable explicit authorization.
column6: Kubelets can be configured to allow all authenticated requests (even anonymous ones) without needing explicit authorization checks from the apiserver. You should restrict this behavior and only allow explicitly authorized requests.
column7: Audit Method 1: Kubelets can accept configuration via a configuration file and in some cases via command line arguments. It is important to note that parameters provided as command line arguments will override their counterpart parameters in the configuration file (see -- config details in the Kubelet CLI Reference for more info, where you can also find out which configuration parameters can be supplied as a command line argument). With this in mind, it is important to check for the existence of command line arguments as well as configuration file entries when auditing Kubelet configuration. Firstly, SSH to each node and execute the following command to find the Kubelet process: ps -ef | grep kubelet The output of the above command provides details of the active Kubelet process, from which we can see the command line arguments provided to the process. Also note the location of the configuration file, provided with the --config argument, as this will be needed to verify configuration. The file can be viewed with a command such as more or less, like so: Internal Only - General sudo less /path/to/kubelet-config.json Verify that Webhook Authentication is enabled. This may be enabled as a command line argument to the kubelet service with --authentication-token-webhook or in the kubelet configuration file via "authentication": { "webhook": { "enabled": true } }. Verify that the Authorization Mode is set to WebHook. This may be set as a command line argument to the kubelet service with --authorization-mode=Webhook or in the configuration file via "authorization": { "mode": "Webhook }. Audit Method 2: It is also possible to review the running configuration of a Kubelet via the /configz endpoint of the Kubernetes API. This can be achieved using kubectl to proxy your requests to the API. Discover all nodes in your cluster by running the following command: kubectl get nodes Next, initiate a proxy with kubectl on a local port of your choice. In this example we will use 8080: kubectl proxy --port=8080 With this running, in a separate terminal run the following command for each node: export NODE_NAME=my-node-name curl http://localhost:8080/api/v1/nodes/${NODE_NAME}/proxy/configz The curl command will return the API response which will be a JSON formatted string representing the Kubelet configuration. Verify that Webhook Authentication is enabled with "authentication": { "webhook": { "enabled": true } } in the API response. Verify that the Authorization Mode is set to WebHook with "authorization": { "mode": "Webhook } in the API response.
column8: Remediation Method 1: If configuring via the Kubelet config file, you first need to locate the file. To do this, SSH to each node and execute the following command to find the kubelet process: ps -ef | grep kubelet The output of the above command provides details of the active kubelet process, from which we can see the location of the configuration file provided to the kubelet service with the --config argument. The file can be viewed with a command such as more or less, like so: sudo less /path/to/kubelet-config.json Enable Webhook Authentication by setting the following parameter: Internal Only - General "authentication": { "webhook": { "enabled": true } } Next, set the Authorization Mode to Webhook by setting the following parameter: "authorization": { "mode": "Webhook } Finer detail of the authentication and authorization fields can be found in the Kubelet Configuration documentation. Remediation Method 2: If using executable arguments, edit the kubelet service file on each worker node and ensure the below parameters are part of the KUBELET_ARGS variable string. For systems using systemd, such as the Amazon EKS Optimised Amazon Linux or Bottlerocket AMIs, then this file can be found at /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubelet-args.conf. Otherwise, you may need to look up documentation for your chosen operating system to determine which service manager is configured: --authentication-token-webhook --authorization-mode=Webhook For Both Remediation Steps: Based on your system, restart the kubelet service and check the service status. The following example is for operating systems using systemd, such as the Amazon EKS Optimised Amazon Linux or Bottlerocket AMIs, and invokes the systemctl command. If systemctl is not available then you will need to look up documentation for your chosen operating system to determine which service manager is configured: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service systemctl status kubelet -l

column3: CIS_Amazon_Elastic_Kubernetes_Service__EKS__Benchmark_v1_5_0_PDF
column4: Ensure that a Client CA File is Configured
column5: Enable Kubelet authentication using certificates.
column6: The connections from the apiserver to the kubelet are used for fetching logs for pods, attaching (through kubectl) to running pods, and using the kubelet’s port-forwarding functionality. These connections terminate at the kubelet’s HTTPS endpoint. By default, the apiserver does not verify the kubelet’s serving certificate, which makes the connection subject to man-in-the-middle attacks, and unsafe to run over untrusted and/or public networks. Enabling Kubelet certificate authentication ensures that the apiserver could authenticate the Kubelet before submitting any requests.
column7: Audit Method 1: Kubelets can accept configuration via a configuration file and in some cases via command line arguments. It is important to note that parameters provided as command line arguments will override their counterpart parameters in the configuration file (see -- config details in the Kubelet CLI Reference for more info, where you can also find out which configuration parameters can be supplied as a command line argument). With this in mind, it is important to check for the existence of command line arguments as well as configuration file entries when auditing Kubelet configuration. Firstly, SSH to each node and execute the following command to find the Kubelet process: ps -ef | grep kubelet The output of the above command provides details of the active Kubelet process, from which we can see the command line arguments provided to the process. Also note the location of the configuration file, provided with the --config argument, as this will be needed to verify configuration. The file can be viewed with a command such as more or less, like so: Internal Only - General sudo less /path/to/kubelet-config.json Verify that a client certificate authority file is configured. This may be configured using a command line argument to the kubelet service with --client-ca-file or in the kubelet configuration file via "authentication": { "x509": {"clientCAFile": } }". Audit Method 2: It is also possible to review the running configuration of a Kubelet via the /configz endpoint of the Kubernetes API. This can be achieved using kubectl to proxy your requests to the API. Discover all nodes in your cluster by running the following command: kubectl get nodes Next, initiate a proxy with kubectl on a local port of your choice. In this example we will use 8080: kubectl proxy --port=8080 With this running, in a separate terminal run the following command for each node: export NODE_NAME=my-node-name curl http://localhost:8080/api/v1/nodes/${NODE_NAME}/proxy/configz The curl command will return the API response which will be a JSON formatted string representing the Kubelet configuration. Verify that a client certificate authority file is configured with "authentication": { "x509": {"clientCAFile": } }" in the API response.
column8: Remediation Method 1: If configuring via the Kubelet config file, you first need to locate the file. To do this, SSH to each node and execute the following command to find the kubelet process: ps -ef | grep kubelet The output of the above command provides details of the active kubelet process, from which we can see the location of the configuration file provided to the kubelet service with the --config argument. The file can be viewed with a command such as more or less, like so: sudo less /path/to/kubelet-config.json Configure the client certificate authority file by setting the following parameter appropriately: Internal Only - General "authentication": { "x509": {"clientCAFile": } }" Remediation Method 2: If using executable arguments, edit the kubelet service file on each worker node and ensure the below parameters are part of the KUBELET_ARGS variable string. For systems using systemd, such as the Amazon EKS Optimised Amazon Linux or Bottlerocket AMIs, then this file can be found at /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubelet-args.conf. Otherwise, you may need to look up documentation for your chosen operating system to determine which service manager is configured: --client-ca-file= For Both Remediation Steps: Based on your system, restart the kubelet service and check the service status. The following example is for operating systems using systemd, such as the Amazon EKS Optimised Amazon Linux or Bottlerocket AMIs, and invokes the systemctl command. If systemctl is not available then you will need to look up documentation for your chosen operating system to determine which service manager is configured: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service systemctl status kubelet -l

column15: The task requires SSH access to each node to locate and verify the kubelet configuration file, which is not directly accessible or manageable through AWS APIs or services like AWS Config or Systems Manager. The kubelet configuration is specific to Kubernetes and not exposed through AWS APIs, making automation infeasible.
column17: 1. **SSH into Each Node:** - Use the command: `ssh ec2-user@<NODE_IP_ADDRESS>` to access each node in your EKS cluster. 2. **Locate the Kubelet Process:** - Execute the command: `ps -ef | grep kubelet`. - This command will show you the active Kubelet process along with its command line arguments and config file path. 3. **Identify the Configuration File:** - Identify the location of the configuration file from the command output, typically specified with the `--config` argument. 4. **View the Kubelet Configuration File:** - If the configuration file is, for example, located at `/path/to/kubelet-config.json`, run: `sudo less /path/to/kubelet-config.json`. 5. **Check the Client CA File Configuration:** - Look for the entry: `"authentication": { "x509": { "clientCAFile": "..." } }` within the kubelet configuration. Ensure that this parameter is set appropriately. 6. **Validate Running Configuration via API:** - Discover all nodes in your cluster by running: `kubectl get nodes`. - Start a proxy with: `kubectl proxy --port=8080`. - For each node, run: `curl http://localhost:8080/api/v1/nodes/<NODE_NAME>/proxy/configz`. - In the returned JSON response, check for the configuration section similar to the kubelet config file and verify that it also includes the `"clientCAFile"` setting. 7. **Confirm Secure Configuration:** - Ensure that the key path for the client CA file is correct and accessible.

column4: Ensure that the --read-only-port is disabled
column5: Disable the read-only port.
column6: The Kubelet process provides a read-only API in addition to the main Kubelet API. Unauthenticated access is provided to this read-only API which could possibly retrieve potentially sensitive information about the cluster.
column7: If using a Kubelet configuration file, check that there is an entry for authentication: anonymous: enabled set to 0. First, SSH to the relevant node: Run the following command on each node to find the appropriate Kubelet config file: ps -ef | grep kubelet The output of the above command should return something similar to --config /etc/kubernetes/kubelet/kubelet-config.json which is the location of the Kubelet config file. Open the Kubelet config file: cat /etc/kubernetes/kubelet/kubelet-config.json Verify that the --read-only-port argument exists and is set to 0. If the --read-only-port argument is not present, check that there is a Kubelet config file specified by --config. Check that if there is a readOnlyPort entry in the file, it is set to 0.
column8: If modifying the Kubelet config file, edit the kubelet-config.json file /etc/kubernetes/kubelet/kubelet-config.json and set the below parameter to 0 Internal Only - General "readOnlyPort": 0 If using executable arguments, edit the kubelet service file /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubelet-args.conf on each worker node and add the below parameter at the end of the KUBELET_ARGS variable string. --read-only-port=0 For each remediation: Based on your system, restart the kubelet service and check status systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service systemctl status kubelet -l
column10: Removal of the read-only port will require that any service which made use of it will need to be re-configured to use the main Kubelet API.
column15: The control requires direct access to the Kubelet configuration files on the EKS worker nodes, which involves SSH access to the EC2 instances. This cannot be automated using AWS APIs or CLI because the specific configuration details are not exposed through AWS services. While AWS Systems Manager could potentially be used to automate some aspects, it would still require manual setup and configuration on each node, making a fully automated solution impractical.
column17: 1. **SSH Access:** SSH into each EKS worker node using the appropriate credentials. ```bash ssh ec2-user@<instance-ip-address> ``` 2. **Locate Kubelet Configuration:** - Execute the command to find the Kubelet process and its configuration file: ```bash ps -ef | grep kubelet ``` - Identify the configuration file path from the output, typically `/etc/kubernetes/kubelet/kubelet-config.json`. 3. **Verify Kubelet Configuration:** - Open the configuration file: ```bash cat /etc/kubernetes/kubelet/kubelet-config.json ``` - Ensure the `readOnlyPort` entry is set to `0`: ```json { "readOnlyPort": 0 } ``` 4. **Examine Executable Arguments:** - If using executable arguments, check the Kubelet service file: ```bash cat /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubelet-args.conf ``` - Confirm the `--read-only-port` argument is set to `0` in the `KUBELET_ARGS` variable. 5. **Restart Kubelet Service (if changes are required):** - Restart the Kubelet service to apply changes: ```bash sudo systemctl restart kubelet ```

column3: CIS_Amazon_Elastic_Kubernetes_Service__EKS__Benchmark_v1_5_0_PDF
column4: Ensure that the --streaming-connection-idle-timeout  argument is not set to 0
column5: Do not disable timeouts on streaming connections.
column6: Setting idle timeouts ensures that you are protected against Denial-of-Service attacks, inactive connections and running out of ephemeral ports. Note: By default, --streaming-connection-idle-timeout is set to 4 hours which might be too high for your environment. Setting this as appropriate would additionally ensure that such streaming connections are timed out after serving legitimate use cases.
column7: Audit Method 1: First, SSH to the relevant node: Run the following command on each node to find the running kubelet process: ps -ef | grep kubelet If the command line for the process includes the argument streaming-connection- idle-timeout verify that it is not set to 0. If the streaming-connection-idle-timeout argument is not present in the output of the above command, refer instead to the config argument that specifies the location of the Kubelet config file e.g. --config /etc/kubernetes/kubelet/kubelet- config.json. Open the Kubelet config file: Internal Only - General cat /etc/kubernetes/kubelet/kubelet-config.json Verify that the streamingConnectionIdleTimeout argument is not set to 0. Audit Method 2: If using the api configz endpoint consider searching for the status of "streamingConnectionIdleTimeout":"4h0m0s" by extracting the live configuration from the nodes running kubelet. Set the local proxy port and the following variables and provide proxy port number and node name; HOSTNAME_PORT="localhost-and-port-number" NODE_NAME="The-Name-Of-Node-To-Extract-Configuration" from the output of "kubectl get nodes" kubectl proxy --port=8001 & export HOSTNAME_PORT=localhost:8001 (example host and port number) export NODE_NAME=ip-192.168.31.226.ec2.internal (example node name from "kubectl get nodes") curl -sSL "http://${HOSTNAME_PORT}/api/v1/nodes/${NODE_NAME}/proxy/configz"
column8: Remediation Method 1: If modifying the Kubelet config file, edit the kubelet-config.json file /etc/kubernetes/kubelet/kubelet-config.json and set the below parameter to a non-zero value in the format of #h#m#s "streamingConnectionIdleTimeout": "4h0m0s" You should ensure that the kubelet service file /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubelet-args.conf does not specify a --streaming-connection-idle-timeout argument because it would override the Kubelet config file. Remediation Method 2: If using executable arguments, edit the kubelet service file /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubelet-args.conf on each worker node and add the below parameter at the end of the KUBELET_ARGS variable string. --streaming-connection-idle-timeout=4h0m0s Remediation Method 3: If using the api configz endpoint consider searching for the status of "streamingConnectionIdleTimeout": by extracting the live configuration from the nodes running kubelet. **See detailed step-by-step configmap procedures in Reconfigure a Node's Kubelet in a Live Cluster, and then rerun the curl statement from audit process to check for kubelet configuration changes Internal Only - General kubectl proxy --port=8001 & export HOSTNAME_PORT=localhost:8001 (example host and port number) export NODE_NAME=ip-192.168.31.226.ec2.internal (example node name from "kubectl get nodes") curl -sSL "http://${HOSTNAME_PORT}/api/v1/nodes/${NODE_NAME}/proxy/configz" For all three remediations: Based on your system, restart the kubelet service and check status systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service systemctl status kubelet -l
column15: The compliance control requires checking the `--streaming-connection-idle-timeout` argument in the Kubelet process and configuration files on EKS nodes. This check involves accessing the node's operating system and examining process arguments and configuration files, which is not directly feasible through AWS APIs or AWS Config. The manual approach is necessary due to the need for SSH access or equivalent to inspect these local configurations.
column17: 1. **SSH to the Node:** - Use SSH to connect to the EKS worker node. - Command: `ssh -i <your-key.pem> ec2-user@<node-ip-address>` 2. **Check the Running Kubelet Process:** - Run the command to find the Kubelet process: - Command: `ps -ef | grep kubelet` - Verify if the output includes the argument `--streaming-connection-idle-timeout`. 3. **Check the Kubelet Configuration File:** - If the argument is not present, check the Kubelet config file for `streamingConnectionIdleTimeout`. - Command: `cat /etc/kubernetes/kubelet/kubelet-config.json` - Verify that the `streamingConnectionIdleTimeout` argument is not set to `0`. 4. **Check Live Configuration via the API:** - Set up a local proxy and extract the Kubelet configuration. - Command: `kubectl proxy --port=8001` - Retrieve the configuration: - Command: `curl -sSL "http://localhost:8001/api/v1/nodes/<node-name>/proxy/configz"` - Ensure `"streamingConnectionIdleTimeout"` is not `0`. 5. **Verify Configuration Changes:** - If modifications are made, restart the Kubelet service: - Command: `sudo systemctl daemon-reload` - Command: `sudo systemctl restart kubelet.service` - Check the status with: - Command: `sudo systemctl status kubelet.service`

column4: Ensure that the --eventRecordQPS argument is set to 0 or a  level which ensures appropriate event capture
column5: Security relevant information should be captured. The eventRecordQPS on the Kubelet configuration can be used to limit the rate at which events are gathered and sets the maximum event creations per second. Setting this too low could result in relevant events not being logged, however the unlimited setting of 0 could result in a denial of service on the kubelet.
column6: It is important to capture all events and not restrict event creation. Events are an important source of security information and analytics that ensure that your environment is consistently monitored using the event data.
column7: Run the following command on each node: sudo grep "eventRecordQPS" /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10- kubeadm.conf Review the value set for the argument and determine whether this has been set to an appropriate level for the cluster. If the argument does not exist, check that there is a Kubelet config file specified by -- config and review the value in this location.
column8: If using a Kubelet config file, edit the file to set eventRecordQPS: to an appropriate level. If using command line arguments, edit the kubelet service file /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf on each worker node and set the below parameter in KUBELET_SYSTEM_PODS_ARGS variable. Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: Internal Only - General systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service
column10: Setting this parameter to 0 could result in a denial of service condition due to excessive events being created. The cluster's event processing and storage systems should be scaled to handle expected event loads.
column15: The task involves verifying the `eventRecordQPS` setting in the Kubelet configuration on EKS worker nodes. This requires accessing configuration files directly on each node, which is not exposed via AWS APIs or CLI tools. While AWS Systems Manager could potentially be used to script this process, it still fundamentally requires manual inspection of configuration files on each node, which is inherently a manual task.
column17: 1. **SSH Access to Worker Nodes:** - Use SSH to connect to each worker node in your EKS cluster. 2. **Locate the Kubelet Configuration:** - Run the command: `sudo grep "eventRecordQPS" /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf`. - If the `eventRecordQPS` argument is not found, proceed to the next step. 3. **Check Kubelet Config File:** - Identify if there is a Kubelet config file specified by the `--config` parameter. - Locate the config file path in the Kubelet service file, typically found at `/etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service`. 4. **Verify the `eventRecordQPS` Value:** - Ensure the `eventRecordQPS` argument exists and verify its value. It should not be set to 0 or an excessively low value that could lead to event loss. 5. **Expected Secure Configuration:** - The value for `eventRecordQPS` should be set to a level that captures necessary events without overwhelming the Kubelet. 6. **Confirm Compliance:** - If adjustments are needed, modify the Kubelet config file or the service file, and restart the Kubelet service using: ``` sudo systemctl daemon-reload sudo systemctl restart kubelet.service ```

column3: CIS_Amazon_Elastic_Kubernetes_Service__EKS__Benchmark_v1_5_0_PDF
column4: Ensure that default service accounts are not actively used.
column5: The default service account should not be used to ensure that rights granted to applications can be more easily audited and reviewed.
column6: Kubernetes provides a default service account which is used by cluster workloads where no specific service account is assigned to the pod. Where access to the Kubernetes API from a pod is required, a specific service account should be created for that pod, and rights granted to that service account. The default service account should be configured such that it does not provide a service account token and does not have any explicit rights assignments.
column7: For each namespace in the cluster, review the rights assigned to the default service account and ensure that it has no roles or cluster roles bound to it apart from the defaults. Additionally ensure that the automountServiceAccountToken: false setting is in place for each default service account.
column8: Create explicit service accounts wherever a Kubernetes workload requires specific access to the Kubernetes API server. Modify the configuration of each default service account to include this value automountServiceAccountToken: false Automatic remediation for the default account: kubectl patch serviceaccount default -p $'automountServiceAccountToken: false' Internal Only - General
column10: All workloads which require access to the Kubernetes API will require an explicit service account to be created.
column15: The compliance control requires checking the configuration of Kubernetes service accounts within an EKS cluster, which is not directly accessible through AWS APIs or boto3. The use of `kubectl` is necessary to interact with Kubernetes resources, as AWS-native tools do not provide direct methods for managing or inspecting Kubernetes service accounts. Therefore, a manual approach using `kubectl` commands is required to verify the configuration of default service accounts.
column17: 1. Open a terminal and ensure you have access to the Kubernetes cluster using `kubectl`. 2. Run `kubectl get serviceaccounts --all-namespaces` to list all service accounts in the cluster. 3. For each namespace, execute `kubectl describe serviceaccount default -n <namespace>` to review the default service account's configuration. 4. Verify that the default service account has no roles or cluster roles bound to it apart from the defaults. 5. Ensure the `automountServiceAccountToken` is set to `false` for each default service account by checking the output of the `kubectl describe` command. 6. Document any deviations and take corrective actions as necessary.
column22: By default the default service account allows for its service account token to be mounted in pods in its namespace.
column27: The compliance control requires checking the configuration of Kubernetes service accounts within an EKS cluster, which is not directly accessible through AWS APIs or boto3. The use of `kubectl` is necessary to interact with Kubernetes resources, as AWS-native tools do not provide direct methods for managing or inspecting Kubernetes service accounts. Therefore, a manual approach using `kubectl` commands is required to verify the configuration of default service accounts.
column29: 1. Open a terminal and ensure you have access to the Kubernetes cluster using `kubectl`. 2. Run `kubectl get serviceaccounts --all-namespaces` to list all service accounts in the cluster. 3. For each namespace, execute `kubectl describe serviceaccount default -n <namespace>` to review the default service account's configuration. 4. Verify that the default service account has no roles or cluster roles bound to it apart from the defaults. 5. Ensure the `automountServiceAccountToken` is set to `false` for each default service account by checking the output of the `kubectl describe` command. 6. Document any deviations and take corrective actions as necessary.

column4: Ensure CNI plugin supports network policies.
column5: There are a variety of CNI plugins available for Kubernetes. If the CNI in use does not support Network Policies it may not be possible to effectively restrict traffic in the cluster.
column6: Kubernetes network policies are enforced by the CNI plugin in use. As such it is important to ensure that the CNI plugin supports both Ingress and Egress network policies.
column7: Review the documentation of CNI plugin in use by the cluster, and confirm that it supports network policies.
column8: As with RBAC policies, network policies should adhere to the policy of least privileged access. Start by creating a deny all policy that restricts all inbound and outbound traffic from a namespace or create a global policy using Calico.
column15: The task requires reviewing the documentation of the CNI plugin in use by the EKS cluster to confirm support for network policies, which is inherently a manual process. While some aspects, such as listing network policies, can be automated, the core requirement of verifying CNI plugin capabilities through documentation cannot be automated using AWS APIs or CLI tools.
column17: 1. **Identify the CNI Plugin:** - Access the EKS cluster configuration through the AWS Management Console or use `kubectl` to determine the CNI plugin in use. - Navigate to the Kubernetes documentation or the specific CNI plugin's official documentation. 2. **Review CNI Plugin Documentation:** - Confirm that the CNI plugin supports both Ingress and Egress network policies by checking its official documentation. 3. **Validate Network Policies:** - Use the Kubernetes CLI to list current network policies: ```bash kubectl get networkpolicies --all-namespaces ``` - Ensure policies are in place that adhere to the principle of least privilege. 4. **Check for Default Deny All Policy:** - Verify that a default deny all policy is implemented by reviewing network policies in relevant namespaces. 5. **Document Findings:** - Record the findings from the documentation review and network policy checks.

column4: Ensure audit logs are collected and managed
column5: Ensure that audit logs are collected and managed in accordance with the enterprise’s audit log management process across all Kubernetes components.
column6: Audit logs provide visibility into the activities occurring within a Kubernetes cluster, enabling the detection and investigation of security incidents and policy violations. Proper collection and management of audit logs are essential for maintaining an audit trail and ensuring compliance with security policies.
column7: 1. Verify audit logging is enabled for Kubernetes components: kubectl get --raw /api/v1/nodes/${NODE_NAME}/proxy/configz | jq '.kubeletConfig.auditPolicy' 2. Ensure the audit logs are being collected and sent to a centralized logging system: kubectl get --raw /api/v1/nodes/${NODE_NAME}/proxy/stats/summary | jq '.auditLogs' 3. Verify that the audit logs are being monitored and managed according to the enterprise’s audit log management process.
column8: 1. Create or update the audit-policy.yaml to specify the audit logging configuration: Internal Only - General apiVersion: audit.k8s.io/v1 kind: Policy rules: - level: Metadata resources: - group: "" resources: ["pods"] 2. Apply the audit policy configuration to the cluster: kubectl apply -f .yaml 3. Ensure audit logs are forwarded to a centralized logging system like CloudWatch, Elasticsearch, or another log management solution: kubectl create configmap cluster-audit-policy --from-file=audit-policy.yaml - n kube-system kubectl apply -f - < Internal Only - General
column10: Implementing comprehensive audit logging may require additional storage and processing resources. Care must be taken to ensure that logs are properly secured and managed to avoid any potential security risks associated with log data.
column15: The compliance control requires direct inspection of Kubernetes-specific audit log configurations using `kubectl`, which are not accessible via AWS APIs, boto3, or AWS CLI. While AWS Config and CloudWatch can be used for centralized log management, the initial verification of audit policies and log generation on Kubernetes nodes must be done manually. This aligns with both AI opinions that emphasize the necessity of manual inspection for certain aspects of the control.
column17: 1. **Access the EKS Cluster:** - Use `kubectl` with appropriate credentials to connect to the EKS cluster. 2. **Verify Audit Logging Configuration:** - For each node, run: ```bash kubectl get --raw /api/v1/nodes/${NODE_NAME}/proxy/configz | jq '.kubeletConfig.auditPolicy' ``` - **What to verify/validate:** Confirm that an audit policy is present and configured according to the enterprise requirements. - **Expected secure configuration:** An audit policy should exist that specifies logging levels and resources that need auditing. 3. **Check Audit Log Collection:** - For each node, run: ```bash kubectl get --raw /api/v1/nodes/${NODE_NAME}/proxy/stats/summary | jq '.auditLogs' ``` - **What to verify/validate:** Ensure audit logs are being generated and collected. 4. **Verify Centralized Log Forwarding:** - Ensure audit logs are forwarded to a centralized logging system like CloudWatch, Elasticsearch, or another log management solution.

column4: Ensure that the kubeconfig file permissions are set to 644 or  more restrictive
column5: If kubelet is running, and if it is configured by a kubeconfig file, ensure that the proxy kubeconfig file has permissions of 644 or more restrictive.
column6: The kubelet kubeconfig file controls various parameters of the kubelet service in the worker node. You should restrict its file permissions to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be writable by only the administrators on the system. It is possible to run kubelet with the kubeconfig parameters configured as a Kubernetes ConfigMap instead of a file. In this case, there is no proxy kubeconfig file.
column7: Method 1 SSH to the worker nodes To check to see if the Kubelet Service is running: sudo systemctl status kubelet The output should return Active: active (running) since.. Run the following command on each node to find the appropriate kubeconfig file: ps -ef | grep kubelet The output of the above command should return something similar to --kubeconfig /var/lib/kubelet/kubeconfig which is the location of the kubeconfig file. Run this command to obtain the kubeconfig file permissions: Internal Only - General stat -c %a /var/lib/kubelet/kubeconfig The output of the above command gives you the kubeconfig file's permissions. Verify that if a file is specified and it exists, the permissions are 644 or more restrictive. Method 2 Create and Run a Privileged Pod. You will need to run a pod that is privileged enough to access the host's file system. This can be achieved by deploying a pod that uses the hostPath volume to mount the node's file system into the pod. Here's an example of a simple pod definition that mounts the root of the host to /host within the pod: apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: file-check spec: volumes: - name: host-root hostPath: path: / type: Directory containers: - name: nsenter image: busybox command: ["sleep", "3600"] volumeMounts: - name: host-root mountPath: /host securityContext: privileged: true Save this to a file (e.g., file-check-pod.yaml) and create the pod: kubectl apply -f file-check-pod.yaml Once the pod is running, you can exec into it to check file permissions on the node: kubectl exec -it file-check -- sh Now you are in a shell inside the pod, but you can access the node's file system through the /host directory and check the permission level of the file: ls -l /host/var/lib/kubelet/kubeconfig Verify that if a file is specified and it exists, the permissions are 644 or more restrictive.
column8: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the each worker node. For example, Internal Only - General chmod 644
column10: Ensuring that the kubeconfig file permissions are set to 644 or more restrictive significantly strengthens the security posture of the Kubernetes environment by preventing unauthorized modifications. This restricts write access to the kubeconfig file, ensuring only administrators can alter crucial kubelet configurations, thereby reducing the risk of malicious alterations that could compromise the cluster's integrity. However, this configuration may slightly impact usability, as it limits the ability for non- administrative users to make quick adjustments to the kubelet settings. Administrators will need to balance security needs with operational flexibility, potentially requiring adjustments to workflows for managing kubelet configurations.
column15: The task involves verifying file permissions on the operating system level of EKS worker nodes, which requires direct access to the nodes. This cannot be achieved through AWS APIs, boto3, or AWS CLI, as these tools do not provide direct access to the file system of EC2 instances running as EKS worker nodes. Both AI opinions agree on the necessity of manual inspection, and the alternative method using a privileged pod still requires manual setup and verification.
column17: 1. **SSH to Each Worker Node:** - Use SSH to connect to each EKS worker node using appropriate credentials. 2. **Verify Kubelet Service:** - Run the command: ```bash sudo systemctl status kubelet ``` - **What to verify:** Ensure the output confirms that the Kubelet service is ‘active (running)’. 3. **Identify Kubeconfig File Path:** - Run the command: ```bash ps -ef | grep kubelet ``` - **What to verify:** Look for the `--kubeconfig` argument and note the path to the kubeconfig file, typically found at `/var/lib/kubelet/kubeconfig`. 4. **Check File Permissions:** - Run the command: ```bash stat -c %a <kubeconfig file path> ``` - **Expected secure configuration:** Confirm that the permissions are `644` or more restrictive (for example, `640` or `600`). 5. **Alternative (Privileged Pod):** - If direct SSH access is unavailable, deploy a privileged pod with hostPath that can check the kubeconfig file permissions on the node.

If the ownership is not set to `root:root`, remediate the issue. - Execute the command: `sudo chown root:root <config_file_path>` (replace with the actual file path). 6. column7: Audit Method 1: Kubelets can accept configuration via a configuration file and in some cases via command line arguments. It is important to note that parameters provided as command line arguments will override their counterpart parameters in the configuration file (see -- config details in the Kubelet CLI Reference for more info, where you can also find out which configuration parameters can be supplied as a command line argument). With this in mind, it is important to check for the existence of command line arguments as well as configuration file entries when auditing Kubelet configuration. Firstly, SSH to each node and execute the following command to find the Kubelet process: ps -ef | grep kubelet The output of the above command provides details of the active Kubelet process, from which we can see the command line arguments provided to the process. Also note the location of the configuration file, provided with the --config argument, as this will be needed to verify configuration. The file can be viewed with a command such as more or less, like so: Internal Only - General sudo less /path/to/kubelet-config.json Verify that Anonymous Authentication is not enabled. This may be configured as a command line argument to the kubelet service with --anonymous-auth=false or in the kubelet configuration file via "authentication": { "anonymous": { "enabled": false }. Audit Method 2: It is also possible to review the running configuration of a Kubelet via the /configz endpoint of the Kubernetes API. This can be achieved using kubectl to proxy your requests to the API. Discover all nodes in your cluster by running the following command: kubectl get nodes Next, initiate a proxy with kubectl on a local port of your choice. In this example we will use 8080: kubectl proxy --port=8080 With this running, in a separate terminal run the following command for each node: export NODE_NAME=my-node-name curl http://localhost:8080/api/v1/nodes/${NODE_NAME}/proxy/configz The curl command will return the API response which will be a JSON formatted string representing the Kubelet configuration. Verify that Anonymous Authentication is not enabled checking that "authentication": { "anonymous": { "enabled": false } is in the API response.
column8: Remediation Method 1: If configuring via the Kubelet config file, you first need to locate the file. To do this, SSH to each node and execute the following command to find the kubelet process: ps -ef | grep kubelet The output of the above command provides details of the active kubelet process, from which we can see the location of the configuration file provided to the kubelet service with the --config argument. The file can be viewed with a command such as more or less, like so: sudo less /path/to/kubelet-config.json Disable Anonymous Authentication by setting the following parameter: Internal Only - General "authentication": { "anonymous": { "enabled": false } } Remediation Method 2: If using executable arguments, edit the kubelet service file on each worker node and ensure the below parameters are part of the KUBELET_ARGS variable string. For systems using systemd, such as the Amazon EKS Optimised Amazon Linux or Bottlerocket AMIs, then this file can be found at /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubelet-args.conf. Otherwise, you may need to look up documentation for your chosen operating system to determine which service manager is configured: --anonymous-auth=false For Both Remediation Steps: Based on your system, restart the kubelet service and check the service status. The following example is for operating systems using systemd, such as the Amazon EKS Optimised Amazon Linux or Bottlerocket AMIs, and invokes the systemctl command. If systemctl is not available then you will need to look up documentation for your chosen operating system to determine which service manager is configured: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service systemctl status kubelet -l

column15: The requirement to verify the Kubelet configuration on EKS worker nodes involves accessing the configuration files and command line arguments directly on the EC2 instances. These configurations are not exposed via AWS APIs, AWS CLI, or AWS Config, making it necessary to perform manual checks. The manual approach is essential due to the need for SSH access to inspect the Kubelet process and its configuration parameters directly.
column17: 1. **SSH into Each EKS Worker Node:** - Use SSH with the appropriate key pair and IAM permissions to access each EC2 instance serving as an EKS worker node. 2. **Identify the Kubelet Process:** - Run the command: ```bash ps -ef | grep kubelet ``` - This command will locate the active Kubelet process and show its command line arguments. 3. **Check Command Line Arguments:** - Review the output of the previous command and confirm that `--anonymous-auth=false` is present in the command line arguments. 4. **Check Kubelet Configuration File (if used):** - If the Kubelet is started with a `--config` argument, note the path to the configuration file. - Open the configuration file using: ```bash sudo less /path/to/kubelet-config.json ``` - Verify that the field `authentication.anonymous.enabled` in the configuration file is set to `false`.

column5: Enable Kubelet authentication using certificates.
column6: The connections from the apiserver to the kubelet are used for fetching logs for pods, attaching (through kubectl) to running pods, and using the kubelet’s port-forwarding functionality. These connections terminate at the kubelet’s HTTPS endpoint. By default, the apiserver does not verify the kubelet’s serving certificate, which makes the connection subject to man-in-the-middle attacks, and unsafe to run over untrusted and/or public networks. Enabling Kubelet certificate authentication ensures that the apiserver could authenticate the Kubelet before submitting any requests.

column4: Ensure that the --streaming-connection-idle-timeout  argument is not set to 0
column5: Do not disable timeouts on streaming connections.
column6: Setting idle timeouts ensures that you are protected against Denial-of-Service attacks, inactive connections and running out of ephemeral ports. Note: By default, --streaming-connection-idle-timeout is set to 4 hours which might be too high for your environment. Setting this as appropriate would additionally ensure that such streaming connections are timed out after serving legitimate use cases.
column7: Audit Method 1: First, SSH to the relevant node: Run the following command on each node to find the running kubelet process: ps -ef | grep kubelet If the command line for the process includes the argument streaming-connection- idle-timeout verify that it is not set to 0. If the streaming-connection-idle-timeout argument is not present in the output of the above command, refer instead to the config argument that specifies the location of the Kubelet config file e.g. --config /etc/kubernetes/kubelet/kubelet- config.json. Open the Kubelet config file: Internal Only - General cat /etc/kubernetes/kubelet/kubelet-config.json Verify that the streamingConnectionIdleTimeout argument is not set to 0. Audit Method 2: If using the api configz endpoint consider searching for the status of "streamingConnectionIdleTimeout":"4h0m0s" by extracting the live configuration from the nodes running kubelet. Set the local proxy port and the following variables and provide proxy port number and node name; HOSTNAME_PORT="localhost-and-port-number" NODE_NAME="The-Name-Of-Node-To-Extract-Configuration" from the output of "kubectl get nodes" kubectl proxy --port=8001 & export HOSTNAME_PORT=localhost:8001 (example host and port number) export NODE_NAME=ip-192.168.31.226.ec2.internal (example node name from "kubectl get nodes") curl -sSL "http://${HOSTNAME_PORT}/api/v1/nodes/${NODE_NAME}/proxy/configz"
column8: Remediation Method 1: If modifying the Kubelet config file, edit the kubelet-config.json file /etc/kubernetes/kubelet/kubelet-config.json and set the below parameter to a non-zero value in the format of #h#m#s "streamingConnectionIdleTimeout": "4h0m0s" You should ensure that the kubelet service file /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubelet-args.conf does not specify a --streaming-connection-idle-timeout argument because it would override the Kubelet config file. Remediation Method 2: If using executable arguments, edit the kubelet service file /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubelet-args.conf on each worker node and add the below parameter at the end of the KUBELET_ARGS variable string. --streaming-connection-idle-timeout=4h0m0s Remediation Method 3: If using the api configz endpoint consider searching for the status of "streamingConnectionIdleTimeout": by extracting the live configuration from the nodes running kubelet. **See detailed step-by-step configmap procedures in Reconfigure a Node's Kubelet in a Live Cluster, and then rerun the curl statement from audit process to check for kubelet configuration changes Internal Only - General kubectl proxy --port=8001 & export HOSTNAME_PORT=localhost:8001 (example host and port number) export NODE_NAME=ip-192.168.31.226.ec2.internal (example node name from "kubectl get nodes") curl -sSL "http://${HOSTNAME_PORT}/api/v1/nodes/${NODE_NAME}/proxy/configz" For all three remediations: Based on your system, restart the kubelet service and check status systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service systemctl status kubelet -l

column4: Ensure that the --make-iptables-util-chains argument is set  to true
column5: Allow Kubelet to manage iptables.
column6: Kubelets can automatically manage the required changes to iptables based on how you choose your networking options for the pods. It is recommended to let kubelets manage the changes to iptables. This ensures that the iptables configuration remains in sync with pods networking configuration. Manually configuring iptables with dynamic pod network configuration changes might hamper the communication between pods/containers and to the outside world. You might have iptables rules too restrictive or too open.

column15: The compliance control requires verification of Kubelet process arguments or configuration files on EKS worker nodes, which necessitates SSH access to each node. AWS APIs, including boto3 and AWS CLI, do not provide direct access to these internal configurations or process arguments. While the Kubernetes API could potentially automate parts of this process, it is not universally enabled or accessible, making manual verification the most reliable method.
column17: 1. **SSH to Each EKS Worker Node:** - Obtain SSH access to each worker node in your EKS cluster. - Execute the command: `ps -ef | grep kubelet` to locate the Kubelet process and its arguments. - Verify that the `--make-iptables-util-chains` argument is present and set to `true`. 2. **Check Kubelet Config File (if used):** - If the Kubelet is started with a `--config` argument, locate the referenced config file (commonly `/etc/kubernetes/kubelet/kubelet-config.json`). - Open the file and verify that `"makeIPTablesUtilChains": true` is set. - Ensure that no command-line argument or config file sets this value to `false`. 3. **Alternative via Kubernetes API (if available):** - If you have access to the Kubelet configz endpoint, use `kubectl proxy` and query the endpoint for each node to inspect the configuration. - Search for the `makeIPTablesUtilChains` setting and confirm it is `true`. 4. **Expected Secure Configuration:** - The Kubelet must have `--make-iptables-util-chains=true` as a command-line argument, or `"makeIPTablesUtilChains": true` in its config file.

column4: Ensure that the cluster-admin role is only used where  required
column5: The RBAC role cluster-admin provides wide-ranging powers over the environment and should be used only where and when needed.
column6: Kubernetes provides a set of default roles where RBAC is used. Some of these roles such as cluster-admin provide wide-ranging privileges which should only be applied where absolutely necessary. Roles such as cluster-admin allow super-user access to perform any action on any resource. When used in a ClusterRoleBinding, it gives full control over every resource in the cluster and in all namespaces. When used in a RoleBinding, it gives full control over every resource in the rolebinding's namespace, including the namespace itself.
column7: Obtain a list of the principals who have access to the cluster-admin role by reviewing the clusterrolebinding output for each role binding that has access to the cluster- admin role. kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o=custom- columns=NAME:.metadata.name,ROLE:.roleRef.name,SUBJECT:.subjects[*].name Review each principal listed and ensure that cluster-admin privilege is required for it.
column8: Identify all clusterrolebindings to the cluster-admin role. Check if they are used and if they need this role or if they could use a role with fewer privileges. Where possible, first bind users to a lower privileged role and then remove the clusterrolebinding to the cluster-admin role : Internal Only - General kubectl delete clusterrolebinding [name]
column10: Care should be taken before removing any clusterrolebindings from the environment to ensure they were not required for operation of the cluster. Specifically, modifications should not be made to clusterrolebindings with the system: prefix as they are required for the operation of system components.
column15: The control requires a detailed assessment of the necessity of `cluster-admin` access for each principal, which involves human judgment and consultation with stakeholders to verify business justifications. While initial data gathering can be automated, the critical evaluation of access necessity cannot be fully automated due to its reliance on organizational context and human decision-making.
column17: 1. **Connect to the EKS Cluster:** Use `kubectl` with appropriate kubeconfig credentials to access your EKS cluster. 2. **List All ClusterRoleBindings:** Execute the command: ```bash kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o=custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,ROLE:.roleRef.name,SUBJECT:.subjects[*].name ``` This will list all clusterrolebindings and identify those granting the `cluster-admin` role. 3. **Identify Principals with `cluster-admin`:** Review the output for all bindings where `ROLE` is `cluster-admin`. Document the associated `SUBJECT` (users, groups, service accounts). 4. **Assess Necessity of Access:** For each principal with `cluster-admin` access, manually verify their business justification and operational need for such privileges. Consult with relevant stakeholders (e.g., cluster owners, security team) to confirm necessity. 5. **Expected Secure Configuration:** Ensure only principals with a documented, justified, and approved need retain `cluster-admin` access. Remove or reassign a less privileged role to all others.

column4: Ensure that default service accounts are not actively used.
column5: The default service account should not be used to ensure that rights granted to applications can be more easily audited and reviewed.
column6: Kubernetes provides a default service account which is used by cluster workloads where no specific service account is assigned to the pod. Where access to the Kubernetes API from a pod is required, a specific service account should be created for that pod, and rights granted to that service account. The default service account should be configured such that it does not provide a service account token and does not have any explicit rights assignments.
column7: For each namespace in the cluster, review the rights assigned to the default service account and ensure that it has no roles or cluster roles bound to it apart from the defaults. Additionally ensure that the automountServiceAccountToken: false setting is in place for each default service account.
column8: Create explicit service accounts wherever a Kubernetes workload requires specific access to the Kubernetes API server. Modify the configuration of each default service account to include this value automountServiceAccountToken: false Automatic remediation for the default account: kubectl patch serviceaccount default -p $'automountServiceAccountToken: false' Internal Only - General
column10: All workloads which require access to the Kubernetes API will require an explicit service account to be created.
column15: The control requires inspecting Kubernetes-specific configurations, such as service account settings and role bindings, which are not directly accessible through AWS APIs, boto3, or AWS CLI. Both AI opinions agree that the audit involves Kubernetes-native tools like `kubectl` for detailed inspection, which supports the decision for a manual approach.
column17: 1. **List all namespaces in the EKS cluster:** - Run: `kubectl get namespaces` - **Verification:** Ensure you have a list of all namespaces present in your EKS cluster. 2. **Inspect the default service account for each namespace:** - Run: `kubectl get serviceaccount default -n <namespace> -o yaml` - **Verification:** Check the `automountServiceAccountToken` setting and ensure it is set to `false`. 3. **Check for role or cluster role bindings to the default service account:** - Run: `kubectl get rolebinding,clusterrolebinding --all-namespaces -o yaml | grep -B 5 'name: default'` - **Verification:** Confirm that no explicit roles or cluster roles are bound to the default service account beyond the defaults provided by Kubernetes. 4. **Confirm compliance:** - Ensure that for the default service account in every namespace, `automountServiceAccountToken` is set to `false`, and there are no non-default role bindings.

column4: Ensure that Service Account Tokens are only mounted  where necessary
column5: Service accounts tokens should not be mounted in pods except where the workload running in the pod explicitly needs to communicate with the API server
column6: Mounting service account tokens inside pods can provide an avenue for privilege escalation attacks where an attacker is able to compromise a single pod in the cluster. Avoiding mounting these tokens removes this attack avenue.
column7: Review pod and service account objects in the cluster and ensure that the option below is set, unless the resource explicitly requires this access. automountServiceAccountToken: false
column8: Regularly review pod and service account objects in the cluster to ensure that the automountServiceAccountToken setting is false for pods and accounts that do not explicitly require API server access.
column10: Pods mounted without service account tokens will not be able to communicate with the API server, except where the resource is available to unauthenticated principals.
column15: The control requires checking the `automountServiceAccountToken` setting, which is a Kubernetes-specific configuration not directly accessible through AWS APIs. While AWS Config can monitor certain EKS configurations, it does not provide direct access to Kubernetes pod or service account settings. Therefore, manual inspection using `kubectl` is necessary to ensure compliance.
column17: 1. **Access the EKS Cluster:** - Use `kubectl` to connect to your EKS cluster. 2. **List All Pods in All Namespaces:** - Execute the command: ```bash kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json | jq '.items[] | {namespace: .metadata.namespace, name: .metadata.name, automountServiceAccountToken: .spec.automountServiceAccountToken}' ``` - Verify that `automountServiceAccountToken` is set to `false` for pods not requiring API server access. 3. **List All Service Accounts in All Namespaces:** - Execute the command: ```bash kubectl get serviceaccounts --all-namespaces -o json | jq '.items[] | {namespace: .metadata.namespace, name: .metadata.name, automountServiceAccountToken: .automountServiceAccountToken}' ``` - Ensure `automountServiceAccountToken` is `false` unless necessary for API server access. 4. **Document Findings:** - Record any pods or service accounts where `automountServiceAccountToken` is not `false` and confirm with application owners if API server access is necessary. 5. **Remediation:** - Update pod specifications or service account definitions to set `automountServiceAccountToken: false` where API server access is not required.

column4: Ensure CNI plugin supports network policies.
column5: There are a variety of CNI plugins available for Kubernetes. If the CNI in use does not support Network Policies it may not be possible to effectively restrict traffic in the cluster.
column6: Kubernetes network policies are enforced by the CNI plugin in use. As such it is important to ensure that the CNI plugin supports both Ingress and Egress network policies.
column7: Review the documentation of CNI plugin in use by the cluster, and confirm that it supports network policies.
column8: As with RBAC policies, network policies should adhere to the policy of least privileged access. Start by creating a deny all policy that restricts all inbound and outbound traffic from a namespace or create a global policy using Calico.
column15: The verification of whether a CNI plugin supports network policies requires consulting external documentation, which cannot be automated through AWS APIs or CLI. While the initial identification of the CNI plugin can be automated, the critical step of confirming support for Ingress and Egress policies relies on human judgment and documentation review, making the overall process manual.
column17: 1. **Identify the CNI Plugin in Use:** - Access your EKS cluster using `kubectl`. - Run the command: `kubectl get daemonset -n kube-system` to identify the CNI plugin, such as `aws-node` for AWS VPC CNI or `calico-node` for Calico. 2. **Verify CNI Plugin Documentation:** - Determine the exact version and type of the CNI plugin from the previous step. - Visit the official documentation or GitHub repository for the identified CNI plugin. - Confirm that the plugin version in use supports both Ingress and Egress Kubernetes Network Policies. 3. **Document Findings:** - Record the CNI plugin name, version, and the link to the official documentation confirming network policy support. - Note any limitations or caveats mentioned in the documentation regarding the use of Network Policies. 4. **Expected Secure Configuration:** - Ensure the CNI plugin in use explicitly supports both Ingress and Egress network policies as configured.
