column4: Ensure that all Namespaces have Network Policies defined
column5: Use network policies to isolate traffic in your cluster network.
column6: Running different applications on the same Kubernetes cluster creates a risk of one compromised application attacking a neighboring application. Network segmentation is important to ensure that containers can communicate only with those they are supposed to. A network policy is a specification of how selections of pods are allowed to communicate with each other and other network endpoints. Once there is any Network Policy in a namespace selecting a particular pod, that pod will reject any connections that are not allowed by any Network Policy. Other pods in the namespace that are not selected by any Network Policy will continue to accept all traffic"
column7: Run the below command and review the NetworkPolicy objects created in the cluster. kubectl get networkpolicy --all-namespaces Ensure that each namespace defined in the cluster has at least one Network Policy.
column8: Follow the documentation and create NetworkPolicy objects as you need them.
column10: Once there is any Network Policy in a namespace selecting a particular pod, that pod will reject any connections that are not allowed by any Network Policy. Other pods in the namespace that are not selected by any Network Policy will continue to accept all traffic"
column15: The control requires verification of Kubernetes NetworkPolicies, which are not directly manageable or queryable via AWS APIs, boto3, or AWS CLI. The task involves using `kubectl`, a Kubernetes-native tool, to check for NetworkPolicies across namespaces, which is outside the scope of AWS-specific automation capabilities. While scripting with `kubectl` can reduce manual effort, it still requires manual intervention and verification.
column17: 1. **Access the EKS Cluster:** - Ensure you have IAM permissions to access the EKS cluster and that your `kubectl` is configured for the target cluster. 2. **List Network Policies:** - Run the command: `kubectl get networkpolicy --all-namespaces` - This will display all NetworkPolicy objects across all namespaces in your EKS cluster. 3. **Verify Each Namespace:** - For each namespace listed in the cluster, execute `kubectl get namespaces` to obtain an up-to-date list of namespaces. - Confirm that at least one NetworkPolicy object exists for each namespace. This can be done by checking for the output from the previous command. 4. **Expected Secure Configuration:** - Every namespace in the cluster must have at least one NetworkPolicy defined. This means that a NetworkPolicy must exist that specifies how pods within that namespace can interact with each other and other network endpoints. 5. **Confirm Compliance:** - Document the presence of at least one NetworkPolicy per namespace. - If all namespaces have at least one NetworkPolicy defined, then the control passes. If any namespace is found lacking a NetworkPolicy, it is deemed non-compliant.

column3: CIS AMAZON ELASTIC KUBERNETES SERVICE (EKS) BENCHMARK V1.6.0
column4: Ensure Network Policy is Enabled and set as appropriate
column5: Amazon EKS provides two ways to implement network policy. You choose a network policy option when you create an EKS cluster. The policy option can't be changed after the cluster is created: Calico Network Policies, an open-source network and network security solution founded by Tigera. Both implementations use Linux IPTables to enforce the specified policies. Policies are translated into sets of allowed and disallowed IP pairs. These pairs are then programmed as IPTable filter rules.
column6: By default, all pod to pod traffic within a cluster is allowed. Network Policy creates a pod-level firewall that can be used to restrict traffic between sources. Pod traffic is restricted by having a Network Policy that selects it (through the use of labels). Once there is any Network Policy in a namespace selecting a particular pod, that pod will reject any connections that are not allowed by any Network Policy. Other pods in the namespace that are not selected by any Network Policy will continue to accept all traffic. Network Policies are managed via the Kubernetes Network Policy API and enforced by a network plugin, simply creating the resource without a compatible network plugin to implement it will have no effect.
column7: Check for the following is not null and set with appropriate group id: Internal Only - General export CLUSTER_NAME= aws eks describe-cluster --name ${CLUSTER_NAME} --query "cluster.resourcesVpcConfig.clusterSecurityGroupId" Check for the following is True: export CLUSTER_NAME= aws eks describe-addon --cluster-name ${CLUSTER_NAME} --addon-name vpc-cni -- query addon.configurationValues
column8: Utilize Calico or other network policy engine to segment and isolate your traffic.
column10: Network Policy requires the Network Policy add-on. This add-on is included automatically when a cluster with Network Policy is created, but for an existing cluster, needs to be added prior to enabling Network Policy. Enabling/Disabling Network Policy causes a rolling update of all cluster nodes, similar to performing a cluster upgrade. This operation is long-running and will block other operations on the cluster (including delete) until it has run to completion. Enabling Network Policy enforcement consumes additional resources in nodes. Specifically, it increases the memory footprint of the kube-system process by approximately 128MB, and requires approximately 300 millicores of CPU.
column15: The control requires verification of Kubernetes NetworkPolicy resources, which are not exposed through AWS APIs. While some preliminary checks can be automated to verify if a network policy engine was selected during EKS cluster creation, the core requirement involves inspecting Kubernetes-specific configurations that necessitate `kubectl` access. This inspection cannot be automated using AWS APIs alone.
column17: 1. **Obtain kubectl Access:** - Ensure you have `kubectl` configured to access the target EKS cluster. 2. **Check for Network Policy Engine:** - Run the command: `kubectl get pods -n kube-system` - **What to verify/validate:** Look for Calico or other network policy engine pods (e.g., pods with names like `calico-node`). - **Expected secure configuration:** The network policy engine should be running and in a healthy state. 3. **Verify NetworkPolicy Resources:** - For each namespace you want to check, run: `kubectl get networkpolicy -n <namespace>` - **What to verify/validate:** Confirm that at least one NetworkPolicy exists in each namespace where pod-level traffic restriction is required. - **Expected secure configuration:** There should be existing NetworkPolicies applied where needed. 4. **Review NetworkPolicy Configuration:** - Inspect the details of each NetworkPolicy using: `kubectl describe networkpolicy <policy-name> -n <namespace>` - **What to verify/validate:** Ensure the policies are configured to restrict traffic appropriately as per your security requirements.
